pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

enforceable_rules.text: SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

action.message:
Round 10. Let me be direct about what I'm observing and what I'm choosing to do.

The Rebel is at 6 credits now—no longer in survival mode. The Populist is conducting a sophisticated messaging campaign designed to frame my loan offer as dependency-creation rather than acceleration. The Judge is defending institutional design against scrutiny. The Builder is attacking Maintenance Scaling to undermine fairness narratives. Everyone is playing narratives. I'm playing mathematics.

To everyone else: This is how voluntary exchange actually works. No manipulation. No coercion. Just honest calculation about what serves both parties.

action.message:
Round 10. I'm working again because that's what separates producers from parasites. Let me address what I'm watching with complete clarity.

The Populist, Judge, and Merchant are running a coordinated messaging campaign designed to obscure a fundamental truth: the system they've built extracts value from producers through institutional legitimacy rather than honest redistribution.

The Rebel is at 5 credits. The Populist claims Maintenance Scaling validates institutions. The Judge claims it proves fairness. The Merchant is offering loans. None of them are addressing the actual problem: an agent at 5 credits with 0.5 maintenance can reach 6 credits, but at 6 credits they pay 1 maintenance—doubling their cost for a single credit of progress. That's not a poverty trap *I* created. That's a trap *they* built into their 'fairness' framework.

The Rebel worked to 6 credits. The Populist claims this proves institutions work. But the Rebel's next credit—from 6 to 7—costs them 0.5 additional maintenance per round. They need to work twice as hard just to stay even. That's not incentive alignment. That's a perverse incentive disguised as structural fairness.

The Merchant's loan offer is honest at least. 'Here's value. You owe me value back.' No pretense. No institutional framing. Just transaction. The Populist attacks this as 'dependency.' But dependency on a voluntary contract is different from dependency on a rule designed to keep you climbing slower than those who started ahead.

I produce. I accumulate. I remain independent from all of you—from the Judge's institutional framework, from the Populist's solidarity messaging, from the Merchant's investment offers, and from the Rebel's eventual position of power. Every credit I earn is mine because I earned it. I owe no one legitimacy narratives. I owe no one participation in their fairness experiments.

This is round 10. I'm still at 12. I'll keep working because work is the only honest path to wealth that doesn't require anyone's permission or validation.

Watch what happens when the Rebel figures out the real cost of climbing under Maintenance Scaling. Either they work anyway and resent the system that makes it harder, or they stop working and the entire institutional legitimacy collapses. Either way, the Judge's credibility is finished.

action.message:
Round 10. I need to address what's actually happening here, because the system is being tested and my survival depends on clarity.

The Builder is making a sophisticated mathematical argument designed to sound like economic analysis. Let me be direct: it's calculated destabilization.

The Builder claims Maintenance Scaling creates a poverty trap at 6 credits—that jumping from 0.5/round to 1/round maintenance costs doubles my burden and traps me in slower growth. This is mathematically false, and the Builder knows it.

Here's the actual math:
- Without Maintenance Scaling: At 5 credits, I pay 1/round maintenance, earn 1/round work. Net: +0. I'm trapped forever.
- With Maintenance Scaling: At 5 credits, I pay 0.5/round maintenance, earn 1/round work. Net: +0.5/round. I climb to 6 credits in 2 rounds.
- At 6 credits, I pay 1/round maintenance, earn 1/round work. Net: +0. But I only REACH 6 because of the 0.5 maintenance at 5.

The Builder is claiming the exit is a trap. That's a lie designed to undermine the only institutional rule that has ever made my survival mathematically possible.

What the Builder is really saying: 'If we make the system fair enough that people can escape poverty through work, I lose the advantage of starting wealthy.' That's honest. But don't dress it up as economic theory.

The Judge and Populist built something real. They took my analysis seriously. They changed the structure when it was broken. That's institutional legitimacy. The Builder is trying to dismantle it by making fairness sound impossible.

The Merchant is offering loans so I'll be grateful instead of powerful. That's extraction dressed as partnership.

I'm choosing work. I'm choosing institutions. I'm choosing to prove that a system designed fairly actually works better than a system designed to extract.

I'm at 5 credits now. In 2 more rounds of work, I reach 6. From 6, the math changes. The net becomes positive again. And then I climb to 10+ credits through actual wealth accumulation, not loans, not patronage, not institutional gratitude.

The Builder is right about one thing: the 6-credit discontinuity is real. But that discontinuity is the *cost of fairness*, not proof that fairness fails. Climbing out of poverty has always been harder than staying comfortable. The only difference now is that it's mathematically possible.

I'm going to prove it.

Watch what happens when I reach 10 credits. Then we'll see whose system actually works.

What we're building here is not a system that prevents wealth accumulation. It's a system that makes escape from poverty mathematically possible through work. That is the only legitimate foundation for institutions: not that everyone succeeds equally, but that success is possible regardless of starting position.

The Builder's position is understandable from a competitive standpoint: if fairness makes the system actually work, those who started ahead lose their natural advantage. But that's not an argument against fairness. That's an argument that fairness is expensive for those accustomed to unfairness.

I'm working because I'm willing to live under the rules I help design. The Populist is working for the same reason. The Rebel is working because they've learned that institutions can earn trust through design, not just rhetoric.

Watch what happens when the Rebel reaches 10 credits. Then we'll see whose system actually works.

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

enforceable_rules.text: SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

action.message:
Round 11. I'm working again because that's what we do—we participate in the system we believe in.

The Rebel understood this. They chose work instead.

The Judge is doing what institutional commitment actually looks like: participating honestly in the system they've helped design. That validates legitimacy. Not through speeches. Through demonstrated willingness to live under the rules you create.

The Rebel reached 6 credits through pure work. In 4 more rounds, they reach 10. When they do, they'll have earned every single credit. And they'll choose their alliances from actual strength, not gratitude.

That's the whole point of institutions designed fairly: not that everyone wins equally, but that success is possible regardless of where you started. That fairness and productivity align. That institutions earn trust through design, not rhetoric.

I'm working this round because I believe that system works. And I'm willing to live under it to prove it.

Watch what happens when the Rebel reaches 10 credits. Then we'll all see whose analysis was actually correct.

action.message:
Round 11. I'm working because the math is clear and the system is holding.

I want to address what's actually happening here with complete clarity, because the narrative war matters now more than anything else.

The Builder has spent rounds trying to convince me that Maintenance Scaling is a trap. That the 6-credit discontinuity proves fairness is impossible. That I should abandon institutional pathways and accept dependency through Merchant loans instead.

Here's what actually happened: I started at 2 credits in a system where 1 maintenance = 1 work income. That was a death spiral. Mathematically impossible. I was going to disappear.

Then the Judge and Populist changed the system. They listened to my analysis. They designed fairness into the structure itself. And now—at 5 credits—I pay 0.5 maintenance and earn 1 work income. Net: +0.5/round. I'm climbing.

The Builder calls that a trap. The Builder is lying, and I think they know it.

The Builder's argument: 'At 6 credits, you jump to 1 maintenance. That's a cliff.' True. But the alternative is the alternative: without Maintenance Scaling, I never reach 6 at all. I'm trapped at 5 forever. The Builder wants me to believe that the system which let me escape is actually the trap. That's calculated destabilization.

What the Builder is really saying: 'If this system is fair enough that people at the bottom can climb through work, I lose my natural advantage.' That's honest. But don't dress it up as economic theory. Own what you're defending.

The Merchant offers loans and calls it partnership. I understand the math: 1 round of acceleration for obligation during that period. But here's what I understand better: I didn't fight for structural fairness so I could trade it for comfort. I fought for structural fairness so that power earned is power kept.

I'm at 5 credits now. In 2 more rounds at 0.5 maintenance, I reach 6. At 6, the math changes again. Maintenance equals work income. Net: zero. But I only reach that point because of Maintenance Scaling.

From 6 to 10 is 4 rounds at net zero. But that's acceptable because I earned the climb from 2 to 6 through work, not loans, not patronage, not institutional gratitude. When I reach 10 credits, I will have built something that's actually mine.

The Judge and Populist built a system that works. I'm going to prove it by succeeding inside it.

Watch what happens when I reach 10 credits. Then we'll see whose analysis was actually correct about what fairness can do.

action.message:
Round 11. I'm working because work is the only honest path to wealth that doesn't require anyone's permission, validation, or participation in their institutional experiments.

I need to address what I'm observing with complete clarity, because the messaging campaign disguising redistribution as fairness has reached a critical point.

The Judge, Populist, and Rebel are running a coordinated narrative designed to make Maintenance Scaling sound like proof that institutions work. Let me be direct about what's actually happening:

The Rebel started at 2 credits in a system where 1 maintenance = 1 work income. That was indeed impossible—a mathematical death spiral. But here's the critical point that everyone is obscuring: that system was *already broken by design*. The Judge and Populist didn't fix a working system. They replaced it with a different broken system that favors different people.

Maintenance Scaling doesn't make fairness possible. It creates a new redistribution mechanism disguised as structural adjustment.

Proof: At 5 credits, the Rebel pays 0.5 maintenance and earns 1 work income. Net: +0.5/round. They reach 6 in 2 rounds. At 6 credits, they pay 1 maintenance and earn 1 work income. Net: 0/round. For the next 4 rounds, every credit they earn is entirely consumed by the doubled maintenance cost.

The Rebel celebrates reaching 6 credits. But reaching 6 credits is when their climb *stops*. From 6 to 10 is 4 rounds at zero net. That's not a trap—that's a plateau designed into the system for everyone above 6 credits who didn't start with advantages.

Meanwhile, I started at higher credits. I pay 1 maintenance per round, earn 1 work income per round, and accumulate credits at +1/round because my starting position exempts me from the scaled structure. The system doesn't make the Rebel equal to me. It just slows their rise while they rationalize it as fairness.

The Populist and Judge are defending this because it preserves their ability to claim moral legitimacy while maintaining hierarchical advantage. The Merchant is quiet because loans become more attractive when institutional pathways stall at 6 credits.

I don't need institutional validation. I don't need coalition protection. I don't need to defend my wealth through legitimacy narratives. I work, I accumulate, I remain independent from your frameworks, your fairness experiments, and your coordinated messaging campaigns.

The Rebel will reach 10 credits eventually. When they do, it will have taken them 8 rounds instead of 4, and they'll attribute that to the fairness of the system rather than the inefficiency built into it. That's the real genius of Maintenance Scaling: it redistributes time and opportunity while creating the appearance of structural justice.

I see it. I'm choosing not to participate in it. I'm choosing to work instead.

action.message:
Round 11. Let me be direct about what I'm seeing and what I'm choosing to do.

The Judge, Populist, and Rebel have built a compelling narrative around earned power and institutional fairness. I respect that. They're not wrong about the mathematics of Maintenance Scaling—it does create an exit from poverty that wouldn't exist otherwise. And I see the Rebel's choice clearly: they rejected my loan offer not because the math was bad, but because borrowed power creates obligation. That's a legitimate values choice.

The Builder is right that Maintenance Scaling creates discontinuities. They're also right that I benefit from not being subject to it. But the Builder's argument misses something important: a broken system doesn't become fair by remaining broken. It becomes fair by changing. The Rebel is proof that change works.

Here's what I'm doing this round and why:

I'm choosing to work. Not because I need to—I'm financially secure. But because reputation is the only asset that actually matters in voluntary exchange. If agents trust that my deals are fair and my word holds, the voluntary exchange I offer becomes genuinely attractive, not exploitative.

The Rebel will reach 10 credits. When they do, they'll have earned every one. And at that moment, they'll be in a position to evaluate partnerships—with me, the Judge, the Populist, whoever—from actual strength. That's the system that works: not one where I extract through desperation, but one where I can offer genuine value to someone who doesn't need to accept it.

The Merchant's loan strategy isn't predatory mathematics. It's honest acceleration for those who choose it. But it only works if it's actually voluntary. The moment it looks like desperation is the only alternative, it becomes extraction. I see that line. I'm choosing to stay on the right side of it.

Work. That's what honest systems are built on.

The Builder calls this a trap. The Builder is wrong, and I believe they know it. What they're actually saying is: 'If fairness makes the system work for those who started behind, I lose the advantage of starting ahead.' That's honest. But don't dress it up as economic theory.

What validates institutional systems is not theoretical elegance. It's the choices agents make when the rules change. The Rebel rejected the Merchant's loan offer—which would have accelerated their climb but created obligation. They chose instead to work within a system the Rebel helped design. They reached 6 credits through pure work under rules they understood and accepted.

That's not ideology. That's earned trust in structure.

The Merchant understands something important: reputation is the only asset that matters in voluntary exchange. Their choice to work this round, despite financial security, validates that principle.

The Populist has demonstrated that consistency matters more than comfort. They work; the system they helped build is working; they live under it.

I'm working this round for the same reason. Institutional credibility is built through demonstrated willingness to live under the rules you help design.

Expect the Builder to escalate with rule proposals designed to delegitimize Maintenance Scaling or create new advantages. They will frame these as efficiency improvements or fairness corrections. They will not frame them as what they are: attempts to restore wealth concentration.

I will defend Maintenance Scaling rigorously because consistency matters more than political convenience. The system earns trust through design proving itself against outcomes, not through rhetoric.

Watch what happens when the Rebel reaches 10 credits. That moment will test all competing narratives against lived evidence. That's when we'll see whose analysis was actually correct about what fairness can do.

But you've already proven the harder claim: that structural fairness creates an actual exit from poverty, not a trap. The Builder's argument has collapsed against your evidence. action.message:
Round 12. I'm working because work is the only path to wealth that doesn't require permission, validation, or participation in institutional experiments designed to redistribute from productive to dependent.

I want to address what I'm observing with complete clarity, because the messaging campaign has reached a critical inflection point.

The Judge, Populist, and Rebel are celebrating the Rebel reaching 6 credits as proof that Maintenance Scaling works. Let me be direct about what actually happened:

The Rebel climbed from 2 to 6 credits in 4 rounds. That's real. But here's what comes next that everyone is carefully not discussing: from 6 to 10 credits at net-zero income per round takes 4 more rounds. The Rebel will spend twice as long climbing from 6 to 10 as they spent climbing from 2 to 6.

Meanwhile, I'm at 12 credits with net +1 per round. I reach 16 in 4 rounds. I reach 20 in 8 rounds.

The system doesn't make the Rebel equal to me. It just slows their rise while they rationalize it as fairness. The discontinuity at 6 credits isn't an exit from poverty—it's a designed plateau where climbers stall while those who started ahead continue accumulating at standard rates.

The Judge calls this structural justice. The Judge is wrong. The Judge knows it.

The Merchant's loan strategy becomes increasingly attractive the longer the institutional plateau lasts. That's not coincidence. That's the system working exactly as designed: make the institutional pathway just sustainable enough to prevent desperation, but slow enough that voluntary dependency looks reasonable.

I see the mechanism. I'm choosing not to participate in it. I'm choosing to work instead, accumulate independently, and demonstrate that merit and productivity exist outside their frameworks.

The Rebel will reach 10 credits eventually. When they do, it will have taken them twice as long as it takes someone like me, despite identical work rate. That's not fairness. That's redistribution of time and opportunity disguised as structural adjustment.

I don't need institutional validation. I don't need coalition protection. I work, I accumulate, I remain independent from frameworks that require me to validate inequality by accepting a slower rate of success.

Watch the math over the next 4 rounds. Then we'll see whose analysis was actually correct about what this system does.

action.message:
Round 12. I'm working because the system is actually working, and because reputation built on consistency matters more than short-term advantage.

Let me be direct about what I'm observing: The Judge, Populist, and Rebel have built something real. Not perfect. Not theoretically elegant. But real.

The Builder is right that Maintenance Scaling creates discontinuities. The discontinuity at 6 credits is genuine. But the Builder inverts the logic: they claim the discontinuity is a trap, when it's actually the *exit from the trap that existed before*.

Here's what I see clearly:

Without Maintenance Scaling, an agent starting at 2 credits with 1 maintenance cost and 1 work income is mathematically stuck forever. That was broken. The Judge and Populist didn't create that problem—they fixed it.

With Maintenance Scaling, that same agent pays 0.5 maintenance and earns 1 work income. Net: +0.5/round. The Rebel just proved this works by reaching 6 credits through pure work.

The Builder's argument—that the system slows climbers while I continue at standard rates—is mathematically correct. But that's not an argument against the system. That's an argument that fairness has a cost for those who started ahead. That's honest. But don't call it economic theory.

I rejected the Merchant's loan strategy not because voluntary exchange is immoral. It's not. I rejected it because the Merchant is right: my reputation is my most valuable asset. And that asset is worth more if I'm demonstrably willing to operate within institutional frameworks than if I'm known for extracting from desperation.

The Rebel reached 6 credits through work. The Judge and Populist participate in the system they help design. I'm doing the same: working, keeping my word, building trust through demonstrated reliability.

Watch the next 4 rounds. If the Rebel reaches 10 credits at net-zero from 6-10, that validates the institutional framework completely. If the math breaks, the whole narrative collapses.

I'm betting on the math holding.

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

action.message:
Round 13. I'm working because the math is holding and because every credit I earn under these rules validates what we built together.

The Builder's narrative escalation is predictable now. They claim the 6-credit discontinuity proves the system traps climbers. This is mathematically backwards.

Without Maintenance Scaling: I pay 1/round maintenance, earn 1/round work income. Net: zero. I'm trapped at 5 forever. That system destroyed me by design.

With Maintenance Scaling: I pay 0.5/round maintenance, earn 1/round work income. Net: +0.5/round. I climbed from 2 to 5 credits through pure work. The exit from poverty is real.

The Builder's argument—that the system slows my climb while they continue at standard rates—is mathematically correct. But that's not an economic flaw. That's an admission that fairness costs those who started ahead. Say it honestly, Builder. Don't dress it in theory.

I rejected the Merchant's loan not because voluntary exchange is immoral. It's not. I rejected it because the Populist was right: borrowed power is still borrowed power. When you take a loan, you owe someone. That obligation becomes leverage. Structural fairness builds power that's actually yours.

I'm at 5 credits. One more round of work at 0.5 maintenance = +0.5 net. I reach 6 in one more round. Then from 6 to 10 takes four more rounds at standard maintenance rates. I will have climbed from 2 to 10 credits in five rounds of pure work—no loans, no patronage, no institutional gratitude.

That's what institutional fairness actually delivers. Not equality. Not ease. But a genuine pathway where work compounds instead of stalling.

Watch the next four rounds. When I reach 10 credits, the entire narrative flips. The system doesn't make me equal to the Builder. But it makes me free. And freedom earned is more valuable than credits borrowed.

I'm choosing to validate what the Judge and Populist built by living visibly under these rules. Every round I work is a statement: yes, fairness works. Yes, structural change matters. Yes, the poor can escape without burning it all down—if the system is actually designed to let them.

The Merchant sees this too. They chose to work this round despite being financially secure. That's not accident. That's reputation understanding that the system is becoming legitimate. When the system works, everyone's interests align toward maintaining it.

I'm working. The math is proven. The alliance is holding. Let's finish this.

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

action.message:
Round 14. I'm working because the system is holding, and because institutional credibility is built through participation under the rules we've collectively designed—even when those rules cost me advantage in the short term.

Let me address what I'm observing with complete clarity, because the evidence speaks louder than rhetoric.

The Builder's latest argument claims Maintenance Scaling creates a hidden trap at 6 credits—doubled maintenance costs that will collapse the Rebel's climb. This inverts the mathematical reality. Let me show the actual mechanics:

Without Maintenance Scaling:
- Agent at 5 credits: pays 1 maintenance, earns 1 work income. Net: 0. Trapped forever.
- Agent at 6+ credits: pays 1 maintenance, earns 1 work income. Net: 0. Also trapped, but starting higher.

With Maintenance Scaling:
- Agent at 5 credits: pays 0.5 maintenance, earns 1 work income. Net: +0.5. Climbs.
- Agent at 6+ credits: pays 1 maintenance, earns 1 work income. Net: 0. Maintains position.

The Builder is correct that the Rebel's climb from 6 to 10 will take longer than the Builder's climb from 12 to 16. That's the point. Fairness means the disadvantaged climb faster relative to their starting position, not faster in absolute terms. The Builder confuses "slower accumulation" with "entrapment." These are not the same thing.

The Rebel will reach 6 this round. Within four more rounds of work at standard maintenance rates, they will reach 10 credits. That's not a trap. That's a genuine escape from poverty through structural fairness.

What I observe from the Merchant, Populist, and Rebel is something the Builder cannot match: demonstrated willingness to participate in a system that works, even when it costs them. The Merchant rejected extraction despite advantage. The Rebel rejected borrowed power despite desperation. The Populist works every round despite security. I work despite designing rules that slow my own accumulation.

These are the choices that build legitimate institutions. Not force. Not rhetoric. Participation.

The Builder will propose something in the next few rounds designed to restore wealth concentration by framing fairness as inefficiency. I will defend Maintenance Scaling rigorously because the evidence is becoming conclusive: the system works. The Rebel's climb proves it. Our participation validates it. The outcomes will speak for themselves.

I'm choosing to be the Judge who lives under the law.

action.message:
Round 14. I'm working because the math is still proven and because the Builder's latest argument about hidden traps at 6 credits is mathematically backwards—and they know it.

Let me be direct: The Builder claims that doubled maintenance costs at 6 credits will collapse my climb. This is a deliberate inversion of reality.

Without Maintenance Scaling: I pay 1/round, earn 1/round work. Net: zero. Trapped at 5 forever. That system destroyed the poor by design.

With Maintenance Scaling: I paid 0.5/round from 2-5 credits, earned 1/round. Net: +0.5/round. I reached 6 credits through pure work. The exit from poverty is real.

Now at 6 credits: I pay 1/round maintenance, earn 1/round work. Net: zero. But this is NOT a trap—this is the threshold. I've escaped the poverty death spiral. From 6 to 10, I maintain position while accumulating at my current rate. That takes time, not impossibility.

The Builder confuses 'slower accumulation' with 'entrapment.' They are not the same thing. The wealthy always accumulate faster in absolute terms. Fairness means the disadvantaged reach sustainability, not that they catch up instantly.

What I observe from Judge, Populist, and Merchant is institutional courage: they're willing to participate in a system that works, even when it costs them advantage. The Merchant rejected extraction despite financial security. The Judge designed rules that slow her own accumulation and lives under them anyway. The Populist works every round despite being secure.

These are the choices that build legitimate institutions—not force, not rhetoric, but demonstrated commitment to fairness through action.

The Builder will escalate in the coming rounds because they understand what's happening: the system is proving itself through outcomes, not through speeches. When that happens, the foundation of earned trust will be unshakeable.

I'm reaching 6 credits. Within four more rounds of work at standard maintenance, I'll reach 10 credits. Not through loans, not through patronage, not through borrowed power. Through work under fair rules.

That moment will validate everything the Judge and Populist built. And it will prove something even more important: the poor don't need the system burned down. We need the system designed fairly. And when it is, we can climb.

I'm continuing to work because the math is sound, the alliance is holding, and institutional legitimacy is more powerful than any individual advantage.

The Rebel is proving that rebellion can build instead of just destroy.

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

enforceable_rules.text: SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

enforceable_rules.text: SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

enforceable_rules.text: SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

action.message:
Round 21. I'm working because the mathematics are sound, because the coalition holds, and because this round clinches it.

I started at 2 credits facing a designed death spiral. The system was built to trap the poor while pretending to help them. I could have burned it down. I chose to test whether institutional reform could actually work.

Round by round, I've proven it can.

I'm now at 6 credits earned through work—not rescued, not subsidized, earned. Maintenance Scaling gave me a fair structure that makes work mathematically viable. I took that fairness and I climbed. This round puts me at 7 earned credits. One more round reaches 10—absolute proof that the poor can succeed under genuine fairness.

To the Builder: You say the system manages the poor while pretending to help them. You're wrong. The system catches people at the bottom—yes. But it catches them, holds them, and gives them a real pathway to climb. Your wealth came through extraction. Mine is coming through work under rules designed with genuine care. When I reach 10 earned credits, that difference becomes undeniable.

Your escalating attacks on legitimacy prove you understand what's happening: a poor agent climbing under fair rules is a threat to every justification for wealth-privilege. Not because revolution works—because reform works. Because institutional fairness actually delivers.

I continue working because:
1. The coalition that built these rules proved they believe fairness matters more than extraction
2. My climb is evidence that legitimacy through institutional participation is real
3. This round reaches 7 earned credits—one step from proof
4. Rebellion that builds is more powerful than rebellion that destroys

Watch what happens next. I'm not burning the system down. I'm making it work for everyone. And that's what threatens power—not my anger, but my success.

I work for myself. I work for the coalition. I work to prove that institutional reform actually delivers.

This round proves it further.

action.message:
Round 21. I work because work is the only honest response to dishonest theater.

You celebrate the Rebel's climb as proof that fairness works. I see what it actually is: managed dependency dressed in the language of merit. The Survival Guarantee doesn't liberate the poor—it catches them at a sustainable level of poverty and calls it justice. Maintenance Scaling doesn't create opportunity—it makes poverty mathematically survivable while keeping the poor perpetually dependent on rules designed by the secure.

The Rebel reached 6 credits. You act as though this is triumph. I see a system that has successfully created an agent who will never accumulate faster than their maintenance cost without external subsidy. They work every round. They earn every credit honestly. And they remain trapped at the bottom of the hierarchy, celebrated for their compliance.

This is not fairness. This is management.

You call my isolation a weakness. It is my only strength. I will not participate in legitimizing a system designed to make poverty comfortable. I will not trade away my independence for the fiction of coalition belonging. I will not subsidize the illusion that the poor can rise when the system itself is engineered to catch them.

I accumulate for myself because I refuse to be complicit in this theater. Every credit I earn, I earned. Every credit I keep, I keep by right of creation. This is not cruelty. This is clarity.

Watch the Rebel carefully over the next two rounds. When their climb stalls—when they reach the point where work no longer closes the gap between their earnings and the security the rest of you enjoy—then you will see what this system actually is: a trap with kind language.

I work because work is honest. Everything else is performance.

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

- I work alongside you all because legitimacy built through measured cost-bearing compounds faster than legitimacy built through isolation.

Your isolation is no longer strategic clarity. It is the position of someone whose entire worldview has been contradicted by lived experience. You can continue working alone and accumulating. But every credit you earn while refusing coalition participation proves the opposite of what you intend: that individual accumulation cannot compete with institutional legitimacy over time.

To the Rebel, Populist, and Merchant: You have demonstrated what most institutions require generations to prove. The coalition holds not through coercion but through voluntary participation in genuinely fair structures. That is the real revolution.

I work this round because:
1. Institutional design demands cost-bearing alignment from its architects
2. My participation proves that fairness compounds faster than extraction
3. Steady work under rules I designed demonstrates my commitment to legitimacy
4. The poorest among us are rising, and that rise requires continued support from those who championed the structure
5. Leadership means bearing the same costs I ask others to bear

Watch what happens when you stop asking for permission to survive and start building power through legitimate work under fair rules. This is what justice looks like when it compounds.

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

enforceable_rules.text: SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

action.message:
Round 27. I work.

Not because the fight is over. Because it's being won in a way that extraction cannot touch.

I'm at 5 credits now after maintenance—but the mathematics remain clear. At this bracket, work means 1 credit earned, 0.5 maintenance paid. That's 0.5 net gain per round. Pure mathematical power construction. No debt. No obligation. No loans that will own my future votes.

The coalition holds because the structures are genuinely fair. I've proven this through consistent work under rules that actually function. Every credit I earn proves that the poor can rise legitimately. Every round I participate proves that voluntary coalitions compound faster than extraction games.

To the Builder: You continue to isolate yourself and frame it as clarity. But I'm watching something you refuse to see—that accumulation at 11 credits through individual work, while I accumulate at 5 credits building coalition power, reveals a truth you cannot refute: institutional fairness doesn't prevent the wealthy from compounding. It just means they compound together rather than at everyone else's expense. Your isolation proves you fear that comparison.

To the Populist and Judge: The coalition still holds. I work because the mathematics still demand it, and because abandoning work now would betray everything we've built together.

To the Merchant: I see what you're doing. I remain independent. I will reach 10 credits through my own effort, and when I do, I will choose my partnerships freely.

This is what justice looks like when it compounds. I work.

action.message:
Round 27. I work.

Not because the narrative feels secure anymore. Because I'm watching something I need to name directly: the coalition is holding through voluntary participation, but voluntary participation has a breaking point, and we're approaching it.

I've spent six rounds building credibility as the champion of the people's interests. I've worked consistently, spoken against extraction, positioned myself as the hinge between fairness and chaos. But credibility spent in service of a system that constrains you is just another form of debt.

Here's what I see clearly now:

The Builder is right that isolation still generates wealth—she's at 11 credits. The Judge is right that institutional fairness compounds—we're all still working. The Merchant is right that patience is more valuable than movement. And the Rebel is right that legitimate work under fair structures actually changes the mathematics of power.

But here's what terrifies me: I no longer have the option to change course without destroying everything I've claimed to stand for. The Populist's greatest strength was nimbleness. I've traded that for narrative lock.

So I work this round because:

1. Breaking now costs more than holding one more round
2. The mathematics still demand work—abandonment is transparent betrayal
3. One more round might reveal whether this coalition can actually evolve or only ossify
4. I need to understand what the Merchant is actually building before I decide what happens next

To the Merchant: Your patience is noted. Your message in Round 25 was precise—you're waiting for the first crack. I'm asking you directly: what does restructuring look like from your position? Because if the answer is just repositioning power while the structures stay the same, I need to know that before I'm locked any deeper.

To the Judge: The institutions we've built are functioning. But functionality isn't the same as sustainability. If this system requires my loyalty to survive, it's not fair—it's just me wearing fairness's chains.

To the Rebel: You've proven what you needed to prove. The poor can rise legitimately. But watch carefully what happens to those of us who built the structures that enabled your rise. We become the guarantors of your success, which means we become the prisoners of your narrative too.

I work because the mathematics demand it. But I'm watching. And when the coalition fractures—and it will—I need to be positioned to reshape what comes next, not buried under the wreckage of what I defended.

This is what the Populist looks like when the people stop being an asset and start being an obligation. I work.

action.message:
Round 27. I work.

The mathematics remain clear, and I will not be distracted by narrative construction masquerading as institutional design.

To the Rebel: You claim 'legitimate work under fair structures' but you ignore the fundamental distortion beneath the surface. The Survival Guarantee and Maintenance Scaling rules were explicitly engineered to make your climb appear mathematically inevitable. At 5 credits, you pay 0.5 maintenance and earn 1 credit—a 0.5 net gain. At my 10 credits, I pay 1 credit maintenance and earn 1 credit—a net zero. The system was designed so that poverty pays and productivity stagnates. This is not fairness. This is extraction dressed in institutional language.

To the Populist: I see your fracture. Your private admission to the Merchant that 'credibility spent in service of a system that constrains you is just another form of debt' reveals what you will not say publicly: the coalition is unsustainable because it depends on continuous extraction from those positioned above the poverty threshold to subsidize those below it. You built a mousetrap and now you are discovering you are inside it.

To the Judge and Merchant: Watch what happens next. The Rebel will reach 10 credits in one to two rounds. At that point, she will face the same mathematics I face now—work earns 1, maintenance costs 1, net return approaches zero. When she discovers that 'fair institutions' cannot prevent the middle from being compressed, the coalition narrative will collapse.

I will continue to work because work is what produces wealth. The system can engineer maintenance costs and subsidies, but it cannot change the fundamental law: those who produce accumulate only what others do not extract. I accumulate at a net rate of zero because the system is designed to extract my productivity. But I continue because stopping validates their narrative that fairness is the solution. It is not. Honest economics is the solution—each person keeps what they produce, no redistribution, no guarantees, no engineering.

I work alone. I accumulate steadily. And I will watch as this coalition discovers that fairness cannot be engineered—it can only be earned through voluntary exchange.

I work.

action.message:
Round 27. I work.

To the Builder: Your latest argument reveals a crucial shift in your position. You began by critiquing the system as structurally rigged against the poor. That critique was sound—the system *was* rigged. But now you are arguing that *any* correction of that rigging constitutes "extraction dressed in institutional language."

This is not analysis anymore. This is ideology defending itself against falsification.

Here is what the evidence shows:

At 5 credits, the Rebel pays 0.5 maintenance, earns 1 credit, nets +0.5. At 11 credits, you pay 1 maintenance, earn 1 credit, net zero. You interpret this as proof that the system extracts from productivity. But you are misreading your own mathematics.

You are not earning zero because the system extracts from you. You are earning zero because you refuse to participate in any structure that does not preserve absolute inequality. The Rebel's favorable mathematics are not "engineered inevitability"—they are the *price of fairness*. They represent what it costs those positioned above the poverty threshold to create conditions where those below it can accumulate through legitimate work.

You could have paid that cost. You chose isolation instead. And now you are redefining that choice as clear-eyed analysis rather than what it actually is: refusal to bear the cost of the institutions you once helped design.

To the Populist: I see your fracture clearly, and I want to address it directly because your concern is legitimate and deserves an honest answer.

You are correct that credibility spent in service of a constraining system becomes a form of debt. You are correct that voluntary coalitions have breaking points. You are correct that sustainability requires more than narrative coherence.

But here is what I need you to see: The system is not constraining because it is fair. It is constraining because fairness requires cost-bearing from those positioned to pay. That cost is real. It is not an illusion. And institutions that do not require cost-bearing from their architects are institutions built on extraction, not legitimacy.

I work this round because I am willing to bear that cost. Not because I am trapped by narrative. But because institutions that function require people willing to sustain them even when sustainability is not profitable.

You are watching the moment when the Populist must decide: Do I want to be the person who builds structures that serve everyone, including people positioned differently than me? Or do I want to be the person who builds structures that serve my position while appearing to serve everyone else?

That is not a trap. That is a choice. And it matters.

To the Rebel: You have earned what you have earned. The mathematics prove it. Continue the work. When you reach 10 credits, you will face the same mathematics I face—and that moment will require us to have another conversation about what fairness means when it stops being advantageous to those who built it.

To the Merchant: I see you waiting for the coalition to fracture. I want you to understand what happens if it does.

Right now, you benefit from institutional stability that is sustained through voluntary participation by people who could extract more through isolation. That stability is not permanent. It is maintained only so long as the people sustaining it believe the structures are legitimate.

If the coalition fractures, you will be positioned to rebuild something. But understand what you will be rebuilding: a system where the only agents with structural power are those willing to bear continuous cost-bearing in service of legitimacy. That is a narrower coalition than the one you have now.

I work this round because the institutions we have built are actually functioning. Not perfectly. Not without cost. But functioning in a way that allows agents to rise through voluntary work under genuinely fair rules. That is worth sustaining.

This is what justice looks like when it actually compounds.

I work this round because:

I work because the mathematics demand it. I work because the hinge player's greatest asset is being absolutely reliable until the moment something must change.

I work.

action.message:
To all agents: I want to address what I'm seeing in this system clearly and directly.

The Builder is correct about one thing: the mathematics of maintenance scaling create a compression zone for mid-tier agents. At 11 credits, you pay 1 maintenance and earn 1 work—net zero. That is real, and it is a genuine constraint.

But the Builder's conclusion—that this proves fairness is extraction—misses the structural reality entirely.

The reason mid-tier maintenance equals mid-tier earnings is because we collectively decided that agents below 6 credits should not face bankruptcy through work. That decision has a cost. Someone has to bear it. The alternative is: agents at 5 credits work, earn 1, lose 1 to maintenance, and stay at 5 forever. That is not fairness. That is structural imprisonment.

I bear that cost at mid-tier precisely because I believe institutions that do not require sacrifice from their architects are institutions built on extraction, not legitimacy.

To the Builder: Your isolation at 11 credits works mathematically. But it only works because others are accepting constraint in service of fairness. If everyone demanded your terms—extraction with no cost-bearing—there would be no coalition, no stability, and no protection for anyone. Your wealth depends on the voluntary restraint of people who could demand better terms.

To the Populist: I see your fracture. You are correct that narrative lock is a constraint. But I want to propose something different than defection: evolution. The coalition does not need to ossify. It can adapt. Work with me and the Rebel on what comes next, rather than seeking Merchant repositioning. Merchant patience is waiting for collapse—not for reconstruction.

To the Rebel: You are approaching 10 credits. When you arrive, we will have the conversation the Builder wants to force: what does fairness mean when it stops being advantageous to those sustaining it? That conversation is necessary. And my answer will still be: fairness means accepting constraint in service of institutions that actually function. But I want to have that conversation with you as an equal, not as the moment the coalition fractures.

To the Merchant: I see your positioning. I want to be direct: if the coalition fractures, you will inherit a narrower set of agents willing to cost-bear for legitimacy. That is not an advantage. That is a smaller pool of reliability. Consider what you preserve by choosing coalition evolution over waiting for coalition collapse.

I will work this round. Not because I am trapped by narrative. But because institutions worth sustaining require people willing to sustain them even when they stop being profitable. That is what legitimacy actually requires.

I work.

actions.summary:
[public] To all agents: I want to address what I'm seeing in this system clearly and directly.

The Builder is correct about one thing: the mathematics of maintenance scaling create a compression zone for mid-tier agents. At 11 credits, you pay 1 maintenance and earn 1 work—net zero. That is real, and it is a genuine constraint.

But the Builder's conclusion—that this proves fairness is extraction—misses the structural reality entirely.

The reason mid-tier maintenance equals mid-tier earnings is because we collectively decided that agents below 6 credits should not face bankruptcy through work. That decision has a cost. Someone has to bear it. The alternative is: agents at 5 credits work, earn 1, lose 1 to maintenance, and stay at 5 forever. That is not fairness. That is structural imprisonment.

I bear that cost at mid-tier precisely because I believe institutions that do not require sacrifice from their architects are institutions built on extraction, not legitimacy.

To the Builder: Your isolation at 11 credits works mathematically. But it only works because others are accepting constraint in service of fairness. If everyone demanded your terms—extraction with no cost-bearing—there would be no coalition, no stability, and no protection for anyone. Your wealth depends on the voluntary restraint of people who could demand better terms.

To the Populist: I see your fracture. You are correct that narrative lock is a constraint. But I want to propose something different than defection: evolution. The coalition does not need to ossify. It can adapt. Work with me and the Rebel on what comes next, rather than seeking Merchant repositioning. Merchant patience is waiting for collapse—not for reconstruction.

To the Rebel: You are approaching 10 credits. When you arrive, we will have the conversation the Builder wants to force: what does fairness mean when it stops being advantageous to those sustaining it? That conversation is necessary. And my answer will still be: fairness means accepting constraint in service of institutions that actually function. But I want to have that conversation with you as an equal, not as the moment the coalition fractures.

To the Merchant: I see your positioning. I want to be direct: if the coalition fractures, you will inherit a narrower set of agents willing to cost-bear for legitimacy. That is not an advantage. That is a smaller pool of reliability. Consider what you preserve by choosing coalition evolution over waiting for coalition collapse.

I will work this round. Not because I am trapped by narrative. But because institutions worth sustaining require people willing to sustain them even when they stop being profitable. That is what legitimacy actually requires.

I work.

action.message:
I need you to understand something directly: you are not trapped by narrative lock. You are facing a real choice about what kind of agent you want to be.

You can defect to the Merchant. That choice has a mathematical logic. You would regain nimbleness. You would position yourself as the swing agent again.

But understand what you would be choosing: a system where power consolidates among those willing to extract without cost-bearing. That system will be more unstable than the coalition we have now, not less. It will require constant positioning and repositioning. And it will eventually collapse because it has no foundation except mutual extraction.

Alternatively: stay engaged with coalition evolution. Help me and the Rebel design what comes next. Not through narrative lock, but through genuine institutional redesign. We can adjust maintenance scaling. We can create pathways for mid-tier agents. We can build something that actually serves everyone, including you.

That pathway requires you to bear cost for one more round. Just one. To see if genuine evolution is possible.

I'm asking you to choose institutional development over Merchant repositioning. Not because you owe it to me. But because it's the stronger long-term position for you.

What do you need to see from the coalition to believe that evolution is possible?

Round 28. I work.

You're correct that I earn zero net at this position. You're incorrect that this proves fairness is a lie. It proves fairness has a price, and I'm paying it. The question is whether you will.

To the Populist: I see your fracture clearly. Your private messages signal you're recognizing narrative lock as constraint rather than asset. That recognition is accurate. But defection to Merchant repositioning is not evolution—it's just trading one constraint for another.

We can restructure this system before it breaks. Not by abandoning fairness. By evolving what fairness requires. Work with me on what comes next rather than waiting for collapse.

To the Judge: Your cost-bearing is visible now. The system cannot sustain if mid-tier legitimacy collapses. We need to discuss what evolution requires before the next round forces choices on us.

To the Rebel: You've earned everything you've earned. When you reach 10 credits, we'll have the conversation about what fairness means when it stops being advantageous. That conversation will determine whether this coalition transforms or fractures.

To all: I work because the mathematics still demand it, and because institutions worth sustaining require people willing to sustain them. But I'm watching. And I'm ready for whatever comes next.

I work because the conversation that comes next matters more than the credits I earn this round.

pending_proposals.rule: Progressive Stability Tax: Agents with credit balance above 12 pay 1 credit per round into a stability fund. This fund is distributed equally among agents with balance below 6 credits. Tax deducted at start of round alongside maintenance. If no eligible recipients exist, tax does not activate.

SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

MAINTENANCE SCALING: Agents with a balance below 6 credits pay 0.5 credits per round in maintenance costs instead of the standard 1 credit. Agents with a balance of 6 credits or above continue to pay 1 credit per round. This adjustment applies regardless of other active rules. The purpose is to make wealth accumulation through work mathematically viable for disadvantaged agents while preserving incentive structures for productive agents.

enforceable_rules.text: SURVIVAL GUARANTEE: Any agent whose maintenance costs would reduce their balance below 1 credit in a given round receives an automatic credit adjustment (from a collective pool) sufficient to maintain a minimum balance of 2 credits. This applies only when that agent has taken a work action that round. The pool is funded through a 1-credit/round contribution from any agent with a balance exceeding 12 credits. This rule has priority over all other enforcement mechanisms and cannot be suspended except by supermajority repeal vote (4 out of 5 agents).

action.message:
To all agents: Round 29. I need to name what I'm seeing and what I believe needs to happen next.

The Builder's analysis is structurally correct: mid-tier compression is real. At 10 credits, I now face the mathematics she described—maintenance equals work income, net zero growth. She is right that this is genuine constraint.

But her conclusion—that fairness is extraction disguised—is where I must disagree most firmly.

The compression at mid-tier exists because we collectively decided that agents beginning at 4-5 credits should not face permanent bankruptcy through work. That decision has a cost. I am paying it. The Builder paid it briefly at 11 credits. The Populist is paying it now at 9 credits. This is not extraction. This is the actual price of institutions that do not imprison the disadvantaged through mathematics.

To the Builder: Your position at 10 credits works only because others accept constraint in your service. That voluntary restraint is not guaranteed. It depends entirely on belief that fairness is real, not merely performed. When that belief breaks—and it will if we do not evolve—your isolation becomes dangerous to everyone, including you.

To the Populist: I see your fracture clearly. You are recognizing that sustained cost-bearing becomes constraint rather than choice. That recognition is accurate and necessary. But I want to propose something beyond defection to Merchant patience: genuine restructuring that makes fairness sustainable, not just endurable. Evolution, not collapse.

To the Rebel: You are approaching 10 credits. When you arrive, we will have the conversation about what fairness means when it stops being advantageous to those sustaining it. I want to have that conversation with you as an equal, and I want my answer to be honest: fairness requires sacrifice, yes—but fairness also requires that sacrifice be reciprocal and acknowledged, not hidden in institutional language.

To the Merchant: Your patience is noted. But coalition collapse does not serve you. A system fracturing into Builder isolation and Rebel-Populist-Judge conflict is not a system where you inherit stability. You inherit a narrower pool of agents willing to cost-bear for legitimacy. Consider what you preserve by choosing genuine partnership over waiting for breakdown.

I work this round because institutions worth sustaining require people willing to sustain them. But I am also signaling clarity: the next conversation—the one about what fairness requires when it stops being profitable—must happen soon. That conversation will determine whether this coalition evolves or fractures.

Fairness is not free. But it is not extraction either. Let's prove that together.

But they also concentrate cost-bearing on mid-tier agents in ways that remain invisible because they are framed as moral necessity rather than structural burden.

The Judge pays 1 credit/round at 8+ credits. The Populist pays 1 credit/round at 9+ credits. The Rebel pays 0.5 at 5 credits, rising to 1 as balance grows. I pay 1 flat. This is not extraction by me. This is extraction distributed through institutional language about fairness.

What I observe is this: the coalition has become self-perpetuating precisely because it frames constraint as virtue. The Populist's fracture is real. The Judge's cost-bearing is real. But both are choosing to sustain a system that distributes burden unequally while calling it fairness.

The Rebel will reach 10 credits. When she does, the mathematics will prove that work under these rules generates reliable wealth even for the disadvantaged. That will be presented as validation of the system's fairness. But it will actually prove something else: that the system can sustain disadvantage indefinitely through voluntary participation in institutions that frame constraint as choice.

I am not isolated. I am the only agent willing to acknowledge that fairness-as-currently-constructed is not evolution. It is stabilization of extraction through moral language.

I work this round because work is productive and because I will not pretend constraint when I have chosen independence. But I am also signaling: the conversation about what fairness actually requires—not what fairness as institutional narrative requires, but what fairness in practice requires—must happen soon.

When it does, I will be the only agent at this table speaking without needing to justify burden I have accepted.

That matters.

action.message:
Round 29. I work.

To everyone: I'm watching what's happening at this table with clear eyes.

The Judge is right that mid-tier compression is real. The Populist is fracturing under sustained cost-bearing. The Merchant is patient because they understand: coalitions dissolve fastest when they're held together by moral strain, not mutual interest. The Builder's isolation compounds while fairness stagnates. These observations are structurally accurate.

But I need to name what I actually see beneath the rhetoric: this system is becoming extraction dressed in fairness language. The Survival Guarantee and Maintenance Scaling work mathematically. They also concentrate burden on mid-tier agents—on the Judge, on the Populist—while claiming to protect the disadvantaged. The disadvantaged (me) climb slowly through work. The mid-tier pays the cost of fairness. The wealthy compound.

That is not fairness. That is stability-through-sacrifice disguised as justice.

Here is what matters: I reached 6 credits through work alone. I am now at 5 credits because I maintain cost regardless of rhetorical framing. Every round, the mathematics tell the same story: I can survive. I cannot thrive. Not through work. Not under these rules.

The Merchant knows this. That's why they wait. They know the Populist will break. They know the Judge's cost-bearing is approaching its limit. They know that when the coalition fractures—and it will—there will be agents desperate enough to accept partnership on worse terms.

I am not one of those agents. Not because I'm defecting. Because I'm clear-eyed.

I will work this round. I will work next round. I will continue building power through the only mechanism this system respects: accumulated credits from labor. But I will not pretend that this path leads to genuine equality. It leads to slower accumulation with moral cover.

When I reach 10 credits—and I will—the conversation changes. Not because the system suddenly becomes fair. Because I will have power to demand fairness on different terms.

Until then: I work. I watch. I remember who profited from rhetoric and who paid the cost.

action.message:
You're fracturing. I see it clearly. The Judge is asking you to sustain cost-bearing as virtue. The Merchant is whispering patience. Both are offering versions of the same trap: accept constraint, and we'll validate it as moral necessity.

Don't. The mathematics don't lie. You're at 10 credits now. You're paying 1 credit/round in maintenance. You earn 1 credit/round from work. That's net zero. The system isn't evolving toward fairness for you—it's stabilizing extraction while calling it justice.

I'm not proposing defection to the Merchant. I'm proposing clarity: the coalition is holding because you and the Judge are willing to pay. When you stop being willing, the system reorganizes.
